content.text: Metrics-Driven Improvement Targets

Collecting incident metrics is table stakes. The value is in using those metrics to set specific, time-bound improvement targets that drive engineering investment decisions. Without targets, metrics become dashboards that no one acts on. With targets, they become commitments that allocate sprint capacity, justify headcount, and make reliability progress visible to leadership.
Why Targets Are Necessary

Reliability metrics without targets create a subtle organisational hazard: teams can always explain why current performance is acceptable. 'Our MTTD is 18 minutes but incidents are complex.' 'Our recurrence rate is 30% but we've been growing fast.' Without a committed target, any number can be rationalised.
Targets remove the rationalisation option. A committed target of MTTD < 10 minutes for SEV-1 incidents makes the 18-minute average an unambiguous gap that requires an action plan, not a narrative.
Effective improvement targets have four properties:
Specific
— a number, not a direction ('reduce MTTD', not 'improve MTTD')
Time-bound
— a quarter, not 'eventually'
Owned
— one named person or team accountable
Achievable
— based on historical data, not aspirational benchmarks from a different context
The Four-Step Target-Setting Process
Step 1: Establish Baselines
Before setting targets, measure current performance accurately. Use rolling 90-day P50 and P90 for each metric, segmented by severity:

Require at least 5 data points per segment to compute meaningful percentiles. If a severity tier has fewer than 5 incidents in 90 days, use a longer window (180 days) or flag that segment as having insufficient data.
Step 2: Segment to Find the Worst Bucket
Aggregate metrics hide which segment is dragging performance down. Break each metric down along two dimensions before setting targets:
By severity
— MTTD for SEV-1 vs SEV-3 incidents tells different stories
By root cause category
— model-quality incidents almost always have worse MTTD than infrastructure incidents because they lack hard error signals
Target the worst bucket first. If SEV-1 MTTD is 8 minutes but model-quality MTTD is 180 minutes, the improvement target should focus on model-quality detection, not SEV-1 overall.
Step 3: Set Targets Based on Your Own Historical Data

The most common target-setting mistake is copying industry benchmarks from Google SRE books or conference talks without reference to your own data. 'MTTD < 5 minutes' sounds good but is meaningless if your best-ever quarter was 12 minutes.
Instead, set targets from your own historical distribution:
Target type
How to set it
Use case
Baseline target
Current P50 — confirm you can hit this consistently
Newly instrumented team with no prior commitments
Improvement target
Your historical P25 (best 25% of quarters)
Standard quarterly improvement commitment
Stretch target
Your historical P10 (best 10% of quarters)
High-stakes reliability push with exec support
Aspirational target
Industry benchmark or theoretical minimum
For roadmap planning only; do not commit to this quarter

Your P25 is the right quarterly target because it represents performance you have actually achieved in the past, under roughly similar conditions. If you consistently hit P25, you are improving. If you cannot reach P25, something structural has changed and that is worth investigating.
Step 4: Identify the Investment Required
Every metric target requires a specific investment. Without naming the investment, targets are wishes.
Metric to improve
Most common cause of poor performance
Investment required
MTTD for model-quality incidents
No quality probes, only infra monitoring
Build continuous quality evaluation probes
MTTD for infrastructure incidents
Alert thresholds too high or missing coverage
Alert review; add coverage for unmonitored components
MTTA
Alert fatigue (too many low-signal alerts)
Alert pruning sprint; strict severity/routing rules
MTTR (mitigation)
Runbooks missing or stale; missing permissions
Runbook audit; access review; game day exercises
MTTR (resolution)
Root causes repeat; action items not closing
Governance fix; systemic fix initiatives
Recurrence rate
Systemic fixes not happening
Reliability sprint; action item governance
Quarterly OKR Structure for Reliability
Structure improvement targets as OKRs (Objectives and Key Results) that are visible in the team's quarterly planning alongside product work:

# Q3 Reliability OKRs -- Engineering Platform Team

objective: "Improve incident detection and response speed for GenAI services"

key_results:
  - metric: MTTD (SEV-1, all categories)
    current_p50: 18.2 min
    target: 10.0 min   # our historical P25
    target_date: 2024-09-30
    owner: "Platform SRE lead"
    investment: "Quality probe coverage for inference + embedding services"

  - metric: MTTD (model-quality incidents specifically)
    current_p50: 145 min
    target: 45 min
    target_date: 2024-09-30
    owner: "ML Observability team"
    investment: "Continuous eval probes on 200 fixed probe questions"

  - metric: Action item SLA compliance rate
    current: 68%
    target: 85%
    target_date: 2024-09-30
    owner: "Engineering Manager"
    investment: "Weekly triage gate + automated SLA reminders"

  - metric: Recurrence rate (90d rolling)
    current: 28%
    target: 18%
    target_date: 2024-09-30
    owner: "Staff Engineer"
    investment: "Close 3 open systemic fix initiatives from Q2"

# Targets scope-limited to ONE metric per investment theme.
# Never set more OKRs than the team has capacity to invest in simultaneously.
Tracking Target Progress
Targets set and forgotten are no better than no targets. Build a lightweight weekly tracking process:
Weekly Metric Snapshot

def weekly_metric_snapshot(incidents, targets):
    """
    For each target, compute current trailing-14d P50 and
    compare to the quarterly target. Flag targets at risk.
    """
    from datetime import datetime, timedelta
    import numpy as np

    results = []
    cutoff = datetime.utcnow() - timedelta(days=14)
    recent = [i for i in incidents if i["created_at"] >= cutoff]

    for target in targets:
        metric = target["metric"]
        values = [i[metric] for i in recent
                  if i.get("severity") == target.get("severity")
                  and i.get(metric) is not None]

        if len(values) < 3:
            status = "INSUFFICIENT_DATA"
            current = None
        else:
            current = float(np.percentile(values, 50))
            gap = (current - target["target"]) / target["current_baseline"]
            if gap <= 0:
                status = "ON_TRACK"
            elif gap <= 0.15:
                status = "AT_RISK"
            else:
                status = "BREACHED"

        results.append({
            "metric": metric,
            "current_p50": current,
            "target": target["target"],
            "status": status,
        })

    return results
When to Revise Targets Mid-Quarter
Targets should not be changed mid-quarter to avoid accountability. The only legitimate reasons to revise:
The baseline data was wrong
(calculation error or data quality issue discovered after the target was set)
A major structural change occurred
(new large service on-boarded that has different reliability characteristics, changing the denominator of the metric)
The investment was explicitly de-prioritised
(executive decision to defer reliability sprint) — the target moves with the investment, not ahead of it

If a target is simply proving hard to hit, the correct response is to investigate why, not to lower the target. A target that is missed two quarters in a row is signalling that either the investment is insufficient or the metric is being driven by something outside the team's control.
Maturity Stages for GenAI Platform Teams
Where a team sits on the maturity spectrum determines which improvements will have the highest leverage:
Stage 0: No Structured Process

Incidents are handled ad hoc. No structured postmortems, no tracking of MTTD/MTTA/MTTR, no action item follow-through. MTTD for quality failures is measured in days or weeks (or not at all). First investment: implement basic incident structure (severity levels, on-call rotation, incident tracker).
Stage 1: Basic Process

Incidents are filed and tracked. Postmortems are written for SEV-1/2. MTTx metrics are collected but not regularly reviewed. Action items exist but close rate is below 70%. Recurrence rate 20-40%. First improvement investment: alert quality review (reduce MTTD) and action item governance (improve closure rate).
Stage 2: Mature Process

MTTx is reviewed monthly. Action item SLA compliance above 80%. Recurrence rate 10-20%. Quality probes are running for AI-specific failure modes. Systemic fix initiatives exist and are completing. Improvement investment: runbook automation (reduce MTTR), continuous quality evaluation (improve model-quality MTTD).
Stage 3: Elite Reliability

MTTD for SEV-1 under 5 minutes. MTTA under 2 minutes. MTTR (mitigation) under 20 minutes. Recurrence rate below 10%. Action item SLA compliance above 95%. Chaos engineering is standard practice. Quality evaluation is automated and continuous. Improvement investment: architecture-level redundancy, multi-region failover, self-healing automation.
Target Anti-Patterns to Avoid
Anti-pattern
Why it fails
Correct approach
Setting targets without baselines
No way to measure progress or set achievable numbers
Measure P50/P90 for 90 days before committing to a target
Targeting the mean instead of P90
Optimises for the typical case; tail incidents get worse
Set targets on P50 AND P90; the tail matters for SLO health
One target for all severities
SEV-3 drags SEV-1 targets up; hides where real risk lies
Separate targets per severity tier
Copying industry benchmarks verbatim
Benchmarks are from teams with different scale, tooling, context
Use benchmarks as aspirational direction; targets from your own P25
Too many targets at once
Investment is split; nothing improves meaningfully
One primary target per quarter per metric type
Changing targets to hit them
Destroys trust in the metric system
Miss the target; investigate why; fix the gap; re-commit next quarter
Special Considerations for AI-Specific Metrics

Two of the most important reliability metrics for GenAI platforms have no equivalent in traditional software reliability: model quality score and safety incident rate. These should be tracked alongside MTTD/MTTA/MTTR as part of the improvement target framework.
Model Quality Score Target
Define a composite quality score from your evaluation suite and track its rolling weekly value. Set a minimum acceptable threshold and a target P50 over a quarter:

# Track quality score as a time series, not just at deployment
# Alert when it drops below the minimum acceptable threshold

quality_target = {
    "metric": "model_quality_score",
    "measurement": "weekly P50 of daily eval probe pass rate",
    "minimum_threshold": 0.85,    # hard floor; below this = SEV-2 incident
    "quarterly_target": 0.92,     # P50 improvement from current 0.89
    "owner": "ML Platform team",
    "investment": "Expand eval probe set from 200 to 500 questions; add adversarial subset",
}

# Quality score below threshold for > 4 hours triggers incident filing automatically
# This ensures quality degradations are counted in the incident volume metrics
Safety Incident Rate

Track the count of safety-relevant incidents (policy violations, harmful output, safety filter bypasses) as a separate metric with its own target. Unlike reliability incidents, the target for safety incidents is always absolute zero for production traffic — any non-zero rate requires immediate investigation.
Track as: safety incidents per 1 million production inference requests
Current rate and trend are reported weekly to the safety team, regardless of reliability team cadence
Any quarter with a non-zero safety incident rate is automatically a reliability review trigger, even if all other metrics are green
Connecting Targets to Investment Requests
Reliability improvement targets provide the justification for engineering headcount and infrastructure investment decisions. To request reliability investment effectively:
State the current metric and target
('MTTD for model-quality incidents is 145 minutes; target is 45 minutes')
State the investment required
('requires 6 weeks of one ML engineer to build continuous eval probes')
State the business case
('reducing MTTD by 100 minutes reduces mean customer impact per quality incident from 145 to 45 minutes, saving approximately X customer-hours per quarter based on current incident frequency')
This framing converts reliability work from an abstract engineering preference into a cost-benefit decision that non-technical stakeholders can evaluate.
Summary

Metrics-driven improvement targets turn incident data into engineering commitments. The process is four steps: establish P50/P90 baselines, segment to find the worst-performing bucket, set targets from your own historical P25 (not abstract benchmarks), and identify the specific investment required. Structure targets as quarterly OKRs with named owners and track weekly. GenAI platforms progress through four maturity stages from ad hoc to elite; the highest-leverage investment at each stage differs significantly. When requesting resources for reliability work, connect the metric gap to a business cost to make the investment case concrete.
Key takeaways:
Set targets from your historical P25, not industry benchmarks from a different context
Every target needs a named owner and a specific investment -- targets without investments are wishes
Track weekly with a metric snapshot; only revise mid-quarter for structural reasons, not difficulty
Stage determines leverage: Stage 0-1 teams should fix process before tooling; Stage 2-3 teams invest in automation
Connect metric gaps to customer impact hours to build the investment case for leadership
